Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface IFloatingBusProvider

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Provides floating bus data for unmapped or undriven memory/I/O reads.

public interface IFloatingBusProvider

Remarks

What is the Floating Bus?

In real Apple II hardware, reading from unmapped memory addresses or undriven I/O locations does not return a fixed value like 0x00 or 0xFF. Instead, the data bus "floats" and returns the last value that was present on the bus from a previous access. This is typically the most recent memory read by either the CPU or the video scanner.

Why It Matters for Emulation:

  • Software Compatibility: Many Apple II programs rely on floating bus behavior for copy protection, hardware detection, and timing-sensitive operations.
  • Video Effects: Some software reads video memory through the floating bus during display refresh cycles to create visual effects or save memory.
  • Emulator Detection: Copy protection routines often test for floating bus behavior to distinguish real hardware from emulators that return zero.

Implementation Strategies:

Different implementations can provide varying levels of accuracy:

StrategyAccuracy vs Performance
LastValueBusTracks the last CPU read/write value. Fast, moderately accurate.
VideoScannerBusReturns the value being scanned by the video circuitry based on cycle timing. Accurate but requires cycle-perfect emulation.
ZeroBusAlways returns 0x00. Fast but breaks compatibility with floating bus-dependent software.
RandomBusReturns random values. Useful for testing robustness but not accurate.

Usage Example: 

// In memory read logic:
public byte Read(ushort address)
{
    if (IsUnmappedAddress(address))
    {
        // Return floating bus value instead of 0
        return _floatingBusProvider.Read();
    }
    return _memory[address];
}

// In I/O space handler:
public byte ReadIO(ushort address)
{
    if (!HasIOHandler(address))
    {
        // Unhandled I/O returns floating bus
        return _floatingBusProvider.Read();
    }
    return IOHandlers[address].Read();
}

Real-World Example: 80-Column Ghost Characters

On an Apple IIe without an 80-column card, enabling 80-column mode causes characters to appear doubled (ghosted) because:

  1. Video scanner alternates reading main memory (even columns) and aux memory (odd columns)
  2. Without aux memory installed, aux reads return the floating bus value
  3. The floating bus contains the character just read from main memory
  4. Result: Each character appears twice: HELLO becomes HHEELLLLOO
An emulator returning 0 for unmapped reads would show spaces instead of doubled characters, breaking visual compatibility.

Thread Safety:

Implementations are not required to be thread-safe. The emulator core should ensure single-threaded access, or provide synchronization at a higher level.

Methods

Read()

Reads the current floating bus value.

byte Read()

Returns

byte

The byte value that would appear on the data bus for an unmapped or undriven read. This is typically the last value placed on the bus by the CPU or video scanner.

Remarks

When to Call: Call this method whenever the emulator needs to read from an address or I/O location that has no active hardware driving the data bus:

  • Unmapped memory regions (e.g., reads beyond installed RAM)
  • Unimplemented I/O addresses (e.g., empty expansion slots)
  • Auxiliary memory reads when no auxiliary memory is installed
  • Peripheral soft switches that don't implement all 16 addresses

Implementation Notes:

Simple implementations may return the last CPU read/write value. More sophisticated implementations can track video scanner activity to return the actual video memory being scanned at the current cycle, which is more accurate but requires cycle-perfect timing simulation.

Performance: This method may be called frequently (millions of times per second in a running emulator), so implementations should be fast. Avoid complex calculations or I/O operations.
